author: Verbum Community
title: The scope of 'world' (kosmos) in John 3:16
content: The Greek word 'kosmos' here is significant. John uses it 78 times in his Gospel, more than any other NT book. In this context, it emphasizes the universal scope of God's love — not limited to Israel but extending to all humanity. The Reformers debated whether 'world' means every individual or the elect from all nations. Calvin himself wrote that God 'invites all indiscriminately to partake of life.'

author: Verbum Community
title: Monogenes: 'Only begotten' or 'One and only'?
content: The KJV translates 'monogenes' as 'only begotten Son.' Modern scholarship argues the word derives from 'monos' (only) + 'genos' (kind/class), meaning 'one of a kind' or 'unique,' not 'only begotten' (which would be 'monogennetos'). The NIV and ESV translate it as 'one and only Son.' This distinction matters theologically: it emphasizes Christ's unique relationship with the Father rather than a mode of origin.

author: Verbum Community
title: The verb 'bara' (create) — exclusive to God
content: The Hebrew verb 'bara' (H1254) is used exclusively with God as its subject in the entire Old Testament. Humans 'make' (asah) or 'form' (yatsar), but only God 'creates' (bara). This theological distinction is deliberate: bara implies creating something genuinely new, not reshaping existing material. It appears 3 times in Genesis 1 (vv. 1, 21, 27) — at the creation of matter, life, and humanity.

author: Verbum Community
title: Three ways to read 'In the beginning'
content: Hebrew 'bereshit' can be parsed three ways: (1) Absolute: 'In the beginning, God created...' — an independent statement of absolute creation. (2) Construct: 'When God began to create...' — making v.1 a temporal clause (cf. NRSV note). (3) Title/summary: v.1 as a heading, with creation proper starting in v.3. Rashi favored option 2; traditional Christian reading favors option 1 (creation ex nihilo).

author: Verbum Community
title: Shepherd imagery in the Ancient Near East
content: Calling God 'my shepherd' was radical. In Mesopotamia and Egypt, 'shepherd' was a royal title — kings were shepherds of their people. By using it for YHWH, David subverts political theology: Israel's true king is not human but divine. The phrase 'I shall not want' (lo echsar) literally means 'I will not lack' — a bold claim given David's own experiences of deprivation in the wilderness.

author: Verbum Community
title: Does God cause evil or work through it?
content: The Greek 'synergei' (works together) is key. Paul doesn't say God causes all things, but that God works all things together for good. The distinction matters pastorally: suffering isn't God's design but God's raw material. Augustine: 'God judged it better to bring good out of evil than to allow no evil to exist.' The qualifier 'for those who love God' limits this promise to believers in covenant relationship.

author: Verbum Community
title: Penal substitution in Isaiah 53
content: Four Hebrew terms describe the Servant's suffering: 'pierced' (chalal — fatally wounded), 'crushed' (daka — ground to powder), 'punishment' (musar — discipline/correction), and 'wounds' (chabburah — welts/bruises). The preposition 'for' (min) indicates cause: 'because of our transgressions.' This is the clearest OT text on vicarious, substitutionary atonement. The Dead Sea Scrolls (1QIsaᵃ) confirm the Masoretic reading.

author: Verbum Community
title: What does 'poor in spirit' mean?
content: Luke's parallel says simply 'blessed are the poor' (Lk 6:20). Matthew adds 'in spirit' (to pneumati). This isn't about financial poverty or low self-esteem. The Hebrew background is 'anawim' — the humble, afflicted ones who depend entirely on God (cf. Psalm 34:6, 40:17). The Qumran community used this exact phrase ('aniyei ruach') in the War Scroll (1QM 14:7). It means spiritual bankruptcy — knowing you have nothing to offer God.

author: Verbum Community
title: 666 and gematria: Nero or universal symbolism?
content: Hebrew gematria of 'Neron Kaisar' (נרון קסר) equals 666. The variant reading 616 in some manuscripts corresponds to the Latin form 'Nero Caesar' (without final nun). This strongly suggests John's original audience understood the reference to Nero. However, 6 also represents incompleteness (falling short of 7, the number of perfection). Triple 6 may symbolize the ultimate human pretension — aspiring to divine trinity (777) but forever falling short.

author: Verbum Community
title: The Kenosis Hymn: pre-Pauline worship
content: Philippians 2:6-11 is widely recognized as a pre-Pauline hymn — early Christian worship predating Paul's letter (c. AD 62). Key phrase: 'morphe theou' (form of God) doesn't mean appearance but essential nature. 'Harpagmos' (something to be grasped/exploited) is a hapax legomenon — it appears only here in the entire NT. Christ didn't exploit his divine status but voluntarily emptied (ekenosen) himself. This is the theological foundation of kenosis theology.

author: Verbum Community
title: Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh — I AM WHO I AM
content: God's self-revelation 'Ehyeh asher Ehyeh' is built on the verb 'hayah' (to be). But Hebrew 'being' is dynamic, not static — it means 'I will be what I will be' or 'I am becoming who I am becoming.' This is not a philosophical statement about existence (Greek ontology) but a covenantal promise of presence. The name YHWH derives from this same root, making God's very name a promise: 'I will be there.'

author: Verbum Community
title: Faith as 'hypostasis' — substance or assurance?
content: The Greek 'hypostasis' (translated 'substance' in KJV, 'assurance' in ESV/NIV) literally means 'standing under' — a foundation or guarantee. In business papyri from the era, it was used for title deeds — proof of ownership. Faith is the 'title deed' of things hoped for. 'Elegchos' (evidence/conviction) means proof that convinces, as in a legal argument. Hebrews defines faith not as blind belief but as confident evidence-based trust.
